title: Poulet à la Moutarde
subtitle: French chicken with Dijon and cream
excerpt: Chicken thighs browned in butter, deglazed with white wine, and finished in a sauce of Dijon mustard and double cream. Sharp, rich, and quick enough for a weeknight.
sourceNotes: Poulet à la moutarde is a classic French bistro dish, particularly associated with Burgundy and Dijon. Documented in Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, and Simone Beck, Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) and Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960).

content.text: The key to this dish is when the mustard goes in. Add it to the pan while the heat is on and it turns bitter; stir it into the cream before the cream goes in and it disperses evenly; this is the correct method. The mustard provides sharpness, the cream provides richness, and the two balance each other.
Use bone-in, skin-on thighs: they have better flavour than breasts and don't dry out during the braise. The skin, browned at the start, makes the pan sauce significantly better.

Season the chicken. Melt the butter in a wide sauté pan over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken skin-side down for 5 to 6 minutes until deep gold. Turn and brown the other side for 3 minutes. Transfer to a plate. Reduce the heat to medium. Cook the shallots in the pan for 5 minutes until softened. Add the thyme and white wine and let it bubble and reduce by half, about 3 minutes.

Stir the mustard into the cream in a small bowl. Return the chicken to the pan skin-side up. Pour the mustard cream around (not over) the chicken. Reduce to a low simmer, cover, and cook for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Check the sauce and season. Serve with rice, buttered noodles, or mashed potato.

items.cause: Heat too high after adding the cream
items.fix: Keep the heat low after the cream goes in — double cream is stable but will split if it boils hard

items.cause: Good Dijon is assertive by nature
items.fix: Reduce to 2 tablespoons for a milder sauce; or add an extra splash of cream to soften it

content.text: Poulet à la moutarde is Burgundian in spirit — the same region that produces both Dijon mustard and the Charolais beef that makes boeuf bourguignon. It is a weeknight dish but also a dinner-party dish: quick to make, elegant in result, and immediately recognisable as French in flavour. The sauce reduces around the chicken during the braise and coats each piece with a glossy, mustardy cream.
